questions.question: Define thrashing in the context of operating systems.
keyPoints: Thrashing is a state where the system spends most of its time swapping pages between main memory and the disk.
It occurs when the paging algorithm frequently brings pages into memory that are immediately replaced, leading to high page fault rates.
This results in low CPU utilization as the CPU spends more time waiting for pages than executing processes.

questions.question: Explain the primary cause of thrashing and a common technique an operating system uses to prevent it.
keyPoints: The primary cause is when the total working set of all active processes exceeds the available physical memory (frames).
This leads to a high page fault rate as processes cannot hold their required working sets in memory.

A common prevention technique is the use of a 'working set model' where the system estimates the number of frames a process needs and only schedules processes that can have their working set resident to avoid over-allocating memory.

questions.question: Describe in detail how the Local Page Replacement Algorithm and the Global Page Replacement Algorithm differ in their approach to page allocation and their respective roles in contributing to or mitigating thrashing.
keyPoints: Local replacement allocates a fixed number of frames to each process; a process can only replace pages from its own set of frames. This prevents one process from taking frames from another, which can help contain thrashing but may lead to inefficient memory use if the allocation is incorrect.

Global replacement allows a process to replace a page from the frames of any process, making more efficient use of memory. However, this can lead to thrashing as a greedy process can take frames away from others, reducing their working set and increasing their page fault rate.

The global method is more susceptible to thrashing because it lacks memory isolation. To mitigate this, the operating system must use a page-fault frequency (PFF) algorithm or working set strategy to dynamically adjust the number of active processes or frames allocated to prevent the sum of working sets from exceeding physical memory.
